advantages: Packages all partial derivatives into one geometric object: direction of steepest ascent and magnitude of maximal increase.
Directly supplies the update direction for gradient-based optimization via −∇L on loss surfaces.
Connects to directional derivatives through dot products: D_u f = ∇f · u for unit u.
Frameworks compute and cache gradients automatically, scaling to millions of parameters with the chain rule.
disadvantages: Describes only local linear behavior—large learning rates can overshoot valleys or saddle structure on loss surfaces.
High-dimensional gradients can be noisy (mini-batch variance) or misleading near flat plateaus and sharp cliffs.
Non-differentiable points (ReLU kinks, discrete ops) require subgradient conventions or surrogate smooth objectives.
Full gradient computation for every parameter each step is expensive; memory-heavy for very large models without tricks like checkpointing.
